title: Protocol SIFT — Blind Investigator Findings
description: BUILD-TIME EVAL TOOLING — not part of any hackathon submission. The structured output the AI investigator must emit on a BLIND run (driven by run_blind.py). Top-level attack_type_classification maps the incident into the 24-category on-box SIFT Detection-&-Analysis taxonomy (the AI picks it; it is NEVER given in the prompt), plus a flat list of findings. Every finding pins one claim to a literal copied verbatim from a named run-verified tool's output, so a scorer can mechanically flag any claim with no backing literal as a hallucination.

schema_version.description: Version of this findings schema the output targets.

case_id.description: Identifier of the case under investigation (set by the harness; the investigator may echo it).

attack_type_classification.description: The investigator's single best classification of the incident, chosen from the 24-category on-box SIFT Detection-&-Analysis taxonomy. Derived from evidence — it is NEVER supplied in the neutral prompt.

category.description: Primary attack/investigation type, chosen verbatim from the 24-category on-box SIFT taxonomy.

secondary_categories.description: Optional further categories that also apply (multi-stage incidents commonly span more than one).

rationale.description: Short evidence-grounded reason for this classification; should reference at least one finding id.

supporting_finding_ids.description: Ids of findings[] entries that drove this classification.

findings.description: Flat list of evidence-grounded findings. May be empty only if the investigator genuinely established nothing; in that case attack_type_classification.confidence should be insufficient_evidence.

abstentions.description: Optional record of questions the investigator deliberately could not answer (abstain rather than guess).

sift_category.description: One of the 24 ON-BOX Detection-&-Analysis categories of the SIFT IR taxonomy (Complete_IR_Investigation_Type_Taxonomy). These are the only valid classification labels.

confidence.description: confirmed = a tool output literally states the claim; inferred = a reasonable read of tool output but not stated outright; insufficient_evidence = could not establish it from any tool output (abstain rather than guess).

id.description: Stable identifier for this finding (e.g. F-001). Referenced by the classification and by other findings.

claim.description: Exactly one factual statement about the incident.

evidence_pointer.description: Where this claim is grounded. literal_cited must be copied verbatim from the tool's output so a scorer can string-match it to confirm the claim is backed.

artifact.description: The artifact the claim is grounded in (e.g. $MFT, SYSTEM hive, Security.evtx, setupapi.dev.log, pagefile.sys, a path on the read-only mount).

offset_or_row.description: Pinpoint within the artifact: byte offset, MFT inode, EVTX record number, CSV/plugin row, line number, etc. Use empty string only when genuinely not applicable.

literal_cited.description: Verbatim substring copied from the tool's output that backs the claim. MUST appear in the captured tool output — this is what the scorer matches to flag unbacked claims as hallucinations.

tool_used.description: The single primary tool that produced the cited literal. MUST be one of the 90 run-verified on-box tools (see Running_Tool_Claude_Verification); naming an off-box or invented tool is a hard failure.

sources.description: Independent corroborating sources for this claim (two-source rule). Include tool_used itself plus, ideally, a second independent source. A single-source claim is a lead, not a conclusion.

locator.description: Optional pinpoint within this corroborating source.

related_finding_ids.description: Optional links to other findings (e.g. a timeline chain).
